title: Pronouns, zijn & hebben
situation: Foundation: the first grammar a beginner needs, subject pronouns plus the two core verbs zijn (to be) and hebben (to have)

phrases.hindi: Ik ben Anna. Ik ben Engels.
phrases.english: I am Anna. I am English.
phrases.context: The two most useful sentences on day one: your name and where you're from. No article before a nationality, just `ik ben Engels`.
phrases.pronunciation: ik BEN AN-na. ik ben ENG-els.

phrases.hindi: Hij is mijn buurman. Zij is mijn buurvrouw.
phrases.english: He is my neighbour (man). She is my neighbour (woman).
phrases.context: `hij` = he, `zij` = she. Both take `is`. Pointing people out is one of the first things you do in a new place.
phrases.pronunciation: hay IS mayn BUUR-man. zay IS mayn BUUR-vrow.

phrases.hindi: Wij zijn moe en jullie zijn klaar.
phrases.english: We are tired and you (plural) are ready.
phrases.context: All three plurals (`wij`, `jullie`, `zij`) use `zijn`. `moe` = tired and `klaar` = ready/done are everyday state words.
phrases.pronunciation: way ZAYN MOO en yul-LEE ZAYN KLAAR.

phrases.hindi: Ik heb honger. Heb jij dorst?
phrases.english: I am hungry (lit. have hunger). Are you thirsty (lit. have thirst)?
phrases.context: Dutch says `honger/dorst hebben` where English says 'to be hungry/thirsty'. Note the dropped `-t`: `heb jij?` not `hebt jij?`.
phrases.pronunciation: ik HEP HONG-er. hep YAY DORST?

phrases.hindi: Zij heeft een auto. Hij heeft een fiets.
phrases.english: She has a car. He has a bicycle.
phrases.context: Possession with `hebben`. Watch the irregular `heeft` for hij/zij/het, not `hebt`.
phrases.pronunciation: zay HAYFT en OW-toh. hay HAYFT en FEETS.

phrases.hindi: Hoe oud ben jij? Ik ben dertig. Mijn zoon is vijf.
phrases.english: How old are you? I am thirty. My son is five.
phrases.context: Dutch uses `zijn` for age the way English does ('I am thirty'), but for hunger and thirst it switches to `hebben`. Mixing these up is a classic beginner slip.
phrases.pronunciation: hoo OWT ben YAY? ik ben DER-tich. mayn ZOHN is FAYF.

phrases.hindi: Bent u de dokter? Ja, dat ben ik.
phrases.english: Are you the doctor? Yes, that's me (lit. that am I).
phrases.context: The formal `u` always takes `bent`. `Dat ben ik` is the natural way to say 'that's me'. Use `u` with a professional you don't know.
phrases.pronunciation: bent UU de DOK-ter? YAA, dat BEN ik.

phrases.hindi: Wij hebben twee kinderen en zij hebben een hond.
phrases.english: We have two children and they have a dog.
phrases.context: Plurals again: `wij hebben`, `zij hebben`. Same `hebben` form for all three plural pronouns, easy to remember.
phrases.pronunciation: way HEB-ben TWAY KIN-der-en en zay HEB-ben en HONT.

grammar_notes: Subject pronouns: ik (I), jij/je (you, informal), u (you, formal), hij (he), zij/ze (she), het (it), wij/we (we), jullie (you all), zij/ze (they). `zij` does double duty for both 'she' and 'they'; the verb form tells them apart (`zij is` = she is, `zij zijn` = they are).

`je` is the unstressed everyday form of `jij`; `jij` is the stressed form you use to point the spotlight at the person, as in `Ik werk hier, en jij?`. Same word, different emphasis. `ze` and `we` are likewise the unstressed forms of `zij` and `wij`.
`zijn` (to be) is fully irregular: ik ben, jij/u bent, hij/zij/het is, and all three plurals are `zijn`. The infinitive `zijn` is identical to the plural form.
`hebben` (to have) is also irregular in the singular: ik heb, jij hebt, hij/zij/het heeft. The plurals are all `hebben`. With formal `u` both `u hebt` and `u heeft` are correct and common.

When `jij` (or `je`) follows the verb in a question, the `-t` drops: `jij hebt` becomes `heb jij?`, `jij bent` becomes `ben jij?`. This dropped `-t` happens only with `jij`/`je`, never with `u`, `hij`, or `zij`.
culture_notes: `jij/je` is informal, `u` is formal. With strangers, older people, officials, your doctor, or a shopkeeper you don't know, default to `u`. With friends, peers, children, and young people, use `je`. When unsure, start with `u`; nobody is offended by being treated politely.

Dutch is becoming steadily more informal, and many people, especially in cities and younger crowds, use `je` quite freely. If someone addresses you with `je` first, it's a friendly cue that you can answer in kind.

Watch out: `zijn` is also the possessive 'his' (`zijn boek` = his book), and `het` is both the pronoun 'it' and the word 'the' for het-words. Context makes the meaning clear, but it surprises beginners.

skill_breakdown.explanation: Nine subject pronouns cover every person. Learn `je` and `ze` and `we` as the casual everyday forms, and reach for `jij`, `zij`, `wij` only when you want to stress the subject.

more_examples.english: We live next to the school.

skill_breakdown.explanation: Use `zijn` for identity, origin, and states: who you are, where you're from, and how you feel. Singular forms are ben/bent/is; all plurals are `zijn`.
Use `hebben` for possession and for body states like hunger, thirst, and being in a hurry. Singular forms are heb/hebt/heeft; all plurals are `hebben`.

more_examples.english: You are right (lit. you have right).
He is in a hurry (lit. has haste).

practice_prompt: You are a friendly, patient Dutch tutor drilling subject pronouns plus the verbs zijn (to be) and hebben (to have) for an absolute beginner. Address the user as `je`. Open with exactly: 'Hoi! Laten we de werkwoorden zijn en hebben oefenen. Eerste vraag: wat is de ik-vorm van zijn?' Give one tiny item at a time and wait for the answer before the next. Mix the task types: ask for a single conjugation (the jullie-form of hebben), give a pronoun plus a state and ask for a full short sentence (`hij` plus moe gives `hij is moe`), or ask which verb fits a meaning (hunger uses hebben, age uses zijn). After each answer, confirm briefly, then give the next item. Watch for the classic errors: `ik bent`, `hij hebt` instead of `hij heeft`, using zijn for hunger, and forgetting the dropped `-t` in `heb jij?`. Mark every mistake with the exact tag [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]] and add one short plain reason. Keep it warm and encouraging, stay in simple Dutch, and after about eight items finish with 'Goed gedaan! Tot de volgende keer.'

theory.intro: Before any other Dutch grammar, you need two things: the words for the people doing the action, and the two verbs you'll reach for in almost every sentence. The pronouns are `ik`, `jij`, `u`, `hij`, `zij`, `het`, `wij`, `jullie`, and `zij` again. The verbs are `zijn` (to be) and `hebben` (to have). Both are irregular, but they're so common that a few days of use will fix them in your memory. Get these, and you can already say who you are, where you're from, how you feel, and what you have.

sections.heading: The pronouns
sections.body:
A subject pronoun is the little word that says who is doing something: `ik` (I), `jij` (you), `hij` (he). Dutch has nine of them, and two pairs of look-alikes to sort out.

First pair: `jij` versus `je`. They mean the same thing, the informal 'you'. `je` is the relaxed everyday form you'll use most of the time. `jij` is the stressed form, for when you want to put the spotlight on the person: `Ik ben klaar, en jij?` (I'm done, and you?). The same goes for `wij`/`we` and `zij`/`ze`, the second word in each pair is just the unstressed everyday version.

Second thing to watch: `zij` does double duty. It means both **she** and **they**. Don't panic, the verb form always tells you which. `zij is` can only be 'she is', and `zij zijn` can only be 'they are'.

And then there's `u`, the polite, formal 'you'. We'll give it its own section later, because choosing between `je` and `u` is a small social skill of its own.

callout.body: In real speech you'll hear `je`, `ze`, and `we` far more than `jij`, `zij`, and `wij`. Save the stressed forms for when you genuinely want to emphasize who you mean.

sections.cutting_intro: Hallo, ik ben Mr. Stroopwafel! Let's meet the people-words first, the pronouns. There are nine, and two little pairs to keep an eye on.

quick_check.question: What does `zij` mean in the sentence `zij zijn moe`?
quick_check.explanation: `zij` means both 'she' and 'they'. Here the plural verb `zijn` tells you it's 'they'. If it were 'she', the verb would be `is` (`zij is moe`).

sections.heading: zijn, to be
sections.body:
`zijn` is the verb 'to be', and it's the single most useful verb in Dutch. You use it for who you are (`Ik ben Anna`), where you're from (`Ik ben Engels`), and how you feel right now (`Ik ben moe`).

It's irregular, so there's no stem rule to lean on. You just memorize the forms. The good news: there are really only four to learn, because all three plurals share one form.

- `ik ben`
- `jij bent` and `u bent`
- `hij`, `zij`, `het` all take `is`
- `wij`, `jullie`, `zij` all take `zijn`

Notice that the plural form `zijn` is spelled exactly like the infinitive. That's not a coincidence to fight, it's a gift: one form covers the dictionary entry and all three plurals.

examples.breakdown: Identity: name. `ik ben` plus the name, no extra word.
Origin: nationality goes straight after `ben`, no article.
State: `hij` plus `is` plus the feeling word.
Plural: `wij zijn`, same form as the infinitive.

callout.body: Unlike English, Dutch puts no article before a profession or nationality: `Ik ben leraar` (I am a teacher), `Ik ben Engels` (I am English). Drop the 'a'.

sections.cutting_intro: Now the big one: `zijn`, the verb 'to be'. It's irregular, but there are only four forms because the plurals all match. Easy.

quick_check.question: Which form of `zijn` goes with `jij`?
quick_check.explanation: `jij bent`, and `u bent` too. `ik` takes `ben`, and `hij/zij/het` take `is`. Don't say `ik bent`, that's the most common slip.

sections.heading: hebben, to have
sections.body:
`hebben` is the verb 'to have', and it's just as essential as `zijn`. You use it for possession (`Ik heb een fiets`, I have a bicycle) and for a whole family of body states where English uses 'to be': `Ik heb honger` (I am hungry, literally 'I have hunger'), `Ik heb dorst` (I am thirsty), `Ik heb haast` (I am in a hurry).

It's irregular too, but the pattern is short:

- `ik heb`
- `jij hebt`
- `hij`, `zij`, `het` take `heeft` (this is the one to memorize)
- `wij`, `jullie`, `zij` all take `hebben`

The trap is `heeft`. A regular verb would give you `hebt` for hij/zij/het, but `hebben` flips to `heeft`. Say it out loud a few times so it becomes automatic.

Dutch also uses `zijn` for age, just like English: `Hoe oud ben jij?` then `Ik ben dertig`. So age is a `zijn` thing, not a `hebben` thing.

examples.breakdown: Possession: `ik heb` plus the thing owned.
Body state: Dutch uses `hebben` where English uses 'to be'. Note `heeft`.
`hij heeft`, the irregular third-person form, not `hij hebt`.
Plural: `wij hebben`, same form for all three plurals.

callout.body: Hunger, thirst, and haste use `hebben` in Dutch, not `zijn`. Say `ik heb honger`, never `ik ben honger`. But age uses `zijn`: `ik ben dertig`. Sort these two buckets early.

sections.cutting_intro: Next: `hebben`, to have. One form to drill harder than the rest: `hij heeft`, not `hij hebt`. Let's lock it in.

quick_check.question: How do you say 'she has a car'?
quick_check.explanation: `zij heeft een auto`. hij/zij/het all take the irregular `heeft`, not `hebt`. `hebben` is the plural form, for `wij`/`jullie`/`zij` (they).

sections.heading: je or u?
sections.body:
Dutch has two ways to say 'you', and choosing between them is a small but real social skill. `je` (or stressed `jij`) is informal. `u` is formal and polite.

The rule of thumb is simple. Use `u` with people you don't know, people older than you, and anyone in a professional role: a doctor, an official at the gemeente, a shopkeeper you've never met. Use `je` with friends, peers, children, and young people.

When you genuinely can't tell, start with `u`. Nobody has ever been offended by being treated politely, and a Dutch person will often invite you to switch by saying something like `je mag je zeggen, hoor` (you can use 'je', you know).

One grammar point ties this section to the last two. `u` always takes the singular verb form, the same one `jij` uses: `u bent`, `u hebt`. So `u` borrows the `jij`-form of the verb, even though it's polite. The only special touch is that `u heeft` is also accepted alongside `u hebt`.

examples.breakdown: `je` with a peer, a classmate, a child.
`u` with a stranger, an official, an older person.
`u bent`, formal, but it uses the same `bent` form as `jij`.
`je` plus dropped `-t` in the inverted question: `heb je?`, not `hebt je?`.

callout.body: When in doubt, use `u`. It's the safe, respectful default with anyone you don't already know. You can always relax into `je` once the other person does.

sections.cutting_intro: Two words for 'you', and picking the right one shows you understand the room. Here's how Dutch people choose between `je` and `u`.

quick_check.question: You're meeting your new doctor for the first time. Which 'you' do you use?
quick_check.explanation: Use `u`. It's the polite form for a professional you don't know. `je` is for friends and peers; `jullie` is the plural 'you all'.

sections.heading: Putting it together
sections.body:
Now you have all the pieces: nine pronouns, the verb `zijn`, the verb `hebben`, and the `je`/`u` choice. Combining them is the whole game at this stage, and the pattern is short.

For a basic sentence, put the pronoun first and the verb second, so pronoun plus verb plus the rest.

- `Ik ben Anna.` (identity)
- `Hij is Nederlands.` (origin)
- `Wij zijn moe.` (state)
- `Ik heb honger.` (body state)
- `Zij heeft een hond.` (possession)

To ask a yes/no question, swap the first two, so verb plus pronoun plus the rest. `Ben jij Engels?` (Are you English?), `Heeft hij tijd?` (Does he have time?). And remember the one quirk from `hebben` and `zijn`: when `jij`/`je` follows the verb, the `-t` drops. `Jij hebt` becomes `Heb je?`, and `Jij bent` becomes `Ben je?`.

That's genuinely enough to introduce yourself, describe people, and ask simple questions. Everything else in Dutch builds on top of this.

examples.breakdown: Pronoun plus `zijn` plus the rest. `ik ben` plus `nieuw hier`.
Pronoun plus `hebben` plus the rest. Note `heeft`, not `hebt`.
Question: verb first, then `jij`. `Jij bent` flips to `Ben jij?`.
Question with dropped `-t`: `je hebt` becomes `Heb je?`.

callout.body: Drill the two questions `Ben je...?` and `Heb je...?` until they're automatic. The dropped `-t` (`heb je`, not `hebt je`) is the detail that makes you sound natural fast.

sections.cutting_intro: Last step: stack the pieces into real sentences. Pronoun, then verb, then the rest. Flip the first two and you've got a question.

quick_check.question: Which is the correct way to ask 'Are you hungry?' informally?
quick_check.explanation: `Heb je honger?`. When `je` follows the verb in a question, the `-t` drops, so it's `heb je`, not `hebt je`. And hunger uses `hebben`, not `zijn`.

theory.wrap_up: You now have the foundation under everything else in Dutch: nine subject pronouns, the verb `zijn` for who you are and how you feel, the verb `hebben` for what you have, and the `je`/`u` choice for matching your tone to the situation. Keep two traps in mind, `hij heeft` (not `hebt`) and the dropped `-t` in `heb je?` / `ben je?`. Memorize hunger and thirst as `hebben` words and age as a `zijn` word. The phrases below mix all of this together so you can practice introducing yourself, describing people, and asking simple questions.
